content_markdown: An object of mass $m$ released from rest near the surface of a planet has an initial acceleration $z$. What is the gravitational field strength near the surface of the planet?
vibe_explanation: By definition, the gravitational field strength $g$ is the force per unit mass ($g = F/m$). According to Newton's Second Law, $F = ma$. For an object in free fall near a planet's surface, the only force acting on it is gravity, so $F = mz$. Substituting this into the field strength formula gives $g = \frac{mz}{m} = z$. Thus, the magnitude of the gravitational field strength is numerically equal to the acceleration of free fall.
